channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Inside the Implosion of Graham Platner’s Maine Senate Campaign: ‘A Slow-Rolling Disaster’
item.description: Graham Platner’s bid for the Senate inspired progressive Democrats. But the campaign, which he suspended Wednesday, was messy, disorganized and ultimately doomed by a steady drip of scandal.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Katie Glueck and Michael Kruse
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Platner Suspends Senate Bid in Maine After Rape Accusation
item.description: Mr. Platner’s withdrawal, which comes after a woman accused him of rape, creates deep uncertainty in a race that both parties see as crucial to their hopes of winning the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner, Lisa Lerer and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Platner’s Exit Sets Off Scramble for New Senate Candidate in Maine
item.description: The state Democratic Party has said it will pick a replacement through a nominating convention before a July 27 deadline. Candidates are already lining up.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Graham Platner and Troy Jackson at a joint campaign rally at the Collins Center for the Arts on the University of Maine campus in May.

item.title: Does a Sex Assault Claim Kill a Political Career? It Depends.
item.description: The swift exit of Graham Platner from the Maine Senate race followed Eric Swalwell’s similar exit from the California governor’s race, but Republicans have taken a different tack.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Democratic allies of Graham Platner called for him to end his campaign for Senate after a woman accused him of rape.

item.title: How the Platner Campaign Imploded, and New Questions About Trump’s Qatari-Donated Jet
item.description: Plus, what Meta’s new A.I. push means for your Instagram pictures.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas and Ian Stewart
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner’s bid for the Senate, which he suspended on Wednesday, was ultimately doomed by a steady drip of scandal.

item.title: Tanker Attacks Risk Overplaying Iran’s Hand, Analysts Say
item.description: Iran’s history shows it is willing to dig in, even when it may suffer big losses.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels on the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday. Traffic last weekend was about one-third of the prewar level of more than 100 ships daily, split evenly between the Iranian and Omani sides of the waterway.

item.title: Inspector of Buckling Midtown Building Cited for Missing Problems at Other NYC Sites
item.description: A New York Times review of city records shows that the firm, Domani Inspection Services, was repeatedly accused of breaking New York City rules.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen, Dionne Searcey, Asmaa Elkeurti and Mihir Zaveri
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: An ambitious building project at 235 East 42nd Street in Manhattan was evacuated suddenly after workers noticed columns beginning to buckle.

item.title: Ukrainians Cautiously Welcome Trump’s Announcement About Air-Defense Systems
item.description: President Trump said the United States would license Ukraine to produce Patriots, which can intercept ballistic missiles. But it could be months or years before those are ready.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine meeting with President Trump on the sidelines of the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: For Ukraine, Other Patriot Makers Are a Cautionary Tale
item.description: Two American allies, Germany and Japan, already have permission to build the American interceptors, a license that President Trump says he will also grant to Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley, Javier C. Hernández and Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Japan’s defense minister, Shinjiro Koizumi, second right, inspecting a Patriot missile interceptor unit in Tokyo in December.

item.title: Trump’s NATO Summit, and Everyone Else’s
item.description: While President Trump insulted allies and demanded loyalty, the military alliance moved quietly closer to accepting more European responsibility for defense.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger
item.media:description: President Trump and the leaders of other NATO countries in Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Security Precaution Led Trump to Use Old Air Force One in Leaving Turkey
item.description: The Secret Service is said to have asked that the president not use the Qatari-donated jet when he left Ankara. The swap deepens questions about the retrofitting of the new plane.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Julian E. Barnes, Eric Schmitt and Eric Lipton
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The older jet used as Air Force One as it was prepared for departure from Ankara, Turkey, on Wednesday.

item.title: Bonnie Tyler, Who Sang ‘Total Eclipse of the Heart,’ Dies at 75
item.description: With a voice both weathered and operatic, she minted 1980s pop anthems like “Total Eclipse” and “Holding Out for a Hero.”
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Adrian Greer Michael Short/Fairfax Media, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bonnie Tyler in 1983. Her biggest hit, “Total Eclipse of the Heart,” became a cultural mainstay that has 1.1 billion streams on Spotify and 1.3 billion views on YouTube.

item.title: Romantasy, Sci-Fi, Ghost Story, High-Seas Adventure: It’s All in the ‘Odyssey’!
item.description: The world’s most famous epic has something for every reader.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott

item.title: A Conservative Role Model for Ending the Fertility Crisis
item.description: Emma Waters, pregnant with her third child, is crafting policy to encourage early parenthood. Some think she is pushing an ideology that does not meet reality.
item.dc:creator: Anemona Hartocollis
item.media:credit: Rebecca Kiger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emma Waters, 28, a policy analyst at the Heritage Foundation, has in a few short years become a rising voice for traditional marriage and family life.

item.title: Can Trump’s Arch Be So Tall? A Panel May Redefine a Law to Get to Yes.
item.description: The proposed 250-foot arch would violate a height limit on Washington structures under the traditional reading of the law. But the panel, now led by the president’s allies, has other ideas.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Emily Badger and Junho Lee
item.media:description: President Trump with a model of the Triumphal Arch last year. The National Capital Planning Commission is set to vote on approval of preliminary site and building plans for the arch on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Says He’ll Ask Supreme Court to Rehear Citizenship Case, an Unlikely Event
item.description: The last time the justices granted a rehearing request after a case decision was in 1965. The court has only once reversed itself after rehearing a case.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump speaking at the White House on Monday.

item.title: As France and Morocco Take the Field, Colonial Past and Global Present Will Mix
item.description: History hangs over the World Cup quarterfinal between France and its former protectorate. These days, national teams intermingle and overlap.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: Morocco fans gathered in Boston on Wednesday, before their match against France.

item.title: A $3.2 Trillion Deal-Making Frenzy Is Spurred by the A.I. Economy
item.description: This year’s boom represents the most spent on global deal-making in a six-month period in a decade. But questions persist about whether it can continue.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The frenzy has heavily favored large companies, with 44 deals announced in the first six months of this year that were larger than $10 billion.

item.title: Renewed Fighting With Iran Shows Cracks in Peace-Trade Rally
item.description: Investors have taken heart from the fragile cease-fire between the United States and Iran before. Will they do so again?
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Ship traffic has slowed drastically in the vital Strait of Hormuz trade conduit as fighting resumed.

item.title: Factory Fire in China Kills 28
item.description: The country’s leader, Xi Jinping, called on the authorities to make every effort to investigate the cause of the blaze in Fujian Province.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:description: In an image released by Chinese state-run news media, firefighters responded at the scene of a shoe factory fire in Jinjiang, a city in southeastern China, on Thursday.

item.title: Big Tech Is Now Targeting Native American Land for Massive Data Centers
item.description: The data center boom has roiled communities across the country, but on Native land, a Big Tech push for quick approvals has pitted the need for development against a history of exploitation.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen and Tamir Kalifa
item.media:description: “True wealth is the well-being of our families,” Chebon Kernell, a tribal council member for the Seminole Nation, said during a tour of his family’s cemetery.

item.title: The Graham Platner Experiment Failed
item.description: The Graham Platner experiment has failed. “There have been so many errors of judgment at every step of the way,” the columnist Michelle Goldberg argues following the implosion of the senate candidate’s campaign in Maine.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg and Stephanie Shen

item.title: The Democrats Can’t Go On Like This
item.description: The trouble in Maine goes beyond a single candidate.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board
item.media:credit: Mark Peterson for The New York Times

item.title: Are We at War Again?
item.description: We look at the state of the conflict with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump

item.title: Late Night Joins the Hunt for Mitch McConnell
item.description: “Apparently, people don’t like a majority senator disappearing with no explanation for three weeks,” Ronny Chieng said on Wednesday’s “Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Sifting Through Venezuela Earthquakes’ Rubble, and the Ashes of a Revolution
item.description: The devastation of Ms. Zúñiga’s city in Venezuela follows the demise of her political project. The revolution is over, but the human ties that help her cope remain.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: Jacqueline Zúñiga, 52, between several public housing complexes that collapsed during the twin earthquake. Ms. Zúñiga’s building was damaged and she is living in a makeshift shelter.
